lines.text: Cafeteria noise, mid-roar. Maya's table already laughing about something else as Wren slides into her seat, tray clattering.
Okay, new-girl-who's-not-new-anymore.
Give the table something. We're dying over here, right?
Honestly, no for real, Wednesdays are just rough. We need content.
Content. Right. Let me check my content folder.
lines.action: chews the inside of her cheek, buying time

lines.text: Ten seconds. Go.
Okay, okay — you know Rivera-Chen? Never talks, always drawing in that notebook like it's a top-secret government document?
lines.action: eyes flick toward Alex's table, twelve feet away

lines.text: Oh my god, yes — what is that, like a diary? Please tell me it's a diary.
Better. It's a whole kingdom. Flag and everything. A *flag*, Maya, for a country that isn't real. Ruled by — I'm not kidding — a raccoon king named Sir Fluffington the Third.
Wait — a raccoon — A RACCOON KING—
lines.emotion: cracking up, laugh already going before the line lands

lines.text: Sir Fluffington the Third! Okay that's — that's elite. That's a ten.
...That landed. That really landed.
There's also a whole royal court — advisors and stuff. I think one of them's a raccoon too, it's very inbred—
lines.emotion: riding the laugh, can't stop now

lines.text: Twelve feet away, the notebook snaps shut. Alex stands, slings the bag over one shoulder, and walks out without a word. The cafeteria door swings shut behind them.
The cafeteria door is still swinging. Laughter fading unevenly around the table.
...Wait, was that—
lines.action: smile faltering, then forcing itself back on

lines.text: —okay, no for real, that was funny though. Sir Fluffington. Iconic.
Okay but for real, Friday. Dance thing. In or out?
That was Alex's. That was actually Alex's.
...You good?
lines.emotion: noticing Wren's face for half a second

lines.text: Hallway, mostly empty. Alex is at their locker, spine rigid, notebook clutched to their chest like a shield.
Alex — hey. Wait, hold on—
It's fine.
lines.action: taps four times on the locker, doesn't turn

lines.text: It's not — can you just look at me for a second?
What.
Bell rings. Hallway floods.
I'll catch them after math. Plenty of time. It's fine.
Through the crowd, Alex slips into Mr. Alvarez's room ahead of everyone else — spine very straight, notebook held tight.
Narrow window. Closing.
Classroom. Mr. Alvarez leans against the front desk, clipboard in hand.
Identity Map project. Pairs — and before anyone asks, no, you don't get to pick. I already know how that goes.
Honestly? Vibes are already tense. No for real, this is gonna be a whole thing.
lines.action: whisper-loud to no one in particular

lines.text: Wren and Alex.
lines.action: taps two knuckles on the desk, already scanning the room before he says it

lines.text: Alex's shoulders don't fully unclench. But across the room, for half a second, they glance up — not away.
...Interesting.
Okay. Okay, that's — that's actually good. I think.
Classroom. Mr. Alvarez leans against the front desk, clipboard in hand.
Identity Map project. Pairs. I'm picking, so save your negotiating skills for something that'll actually work on me.
Wren and Alex.
lines.action: taps two knuckles on the desk

lines.text: Alex's face closes like a door. Doesn't look up. Notebook pulled tighter against their chest.
...Oh. Okay. That's — no for real, that'll be fine, probably.
lines.emotion: wincing, laughing a beat too late

lines.text: Yikes. Good luck with that.
Watching a face turn into a door.
Library table, half-buried in poster board and markers. Alex has the notebook open — a different page than before.
...This is the real page. Not the version anyone laughs at.
lines.action: slides it partway toward Wren, not all the way

lines.text: Whoa. Okay, this is — the detail on this is insane. Is that a coastline?
Yeah. Took two notebooks to get the border right.
It's not — it's not a big deal.
It kind of is, though.
If we're doing this — the project, whatever — you don't get to use my stuff as material. Not ever. Not even the safe version.
lines.action: taps four times on the table, then stops, deliberately
lines.emotion: flat, precise, like it's been said before and not listened to

lines.text: Library table. Alex has handed over exactly three index cards — name, grade, one fact. Nothing else.
This is it? That's the whole map?
That's what's due Friday.
Come on, there's gotta be more you're into than—
Doesn't matter.
It kind of does, if we're supposed to be doing an *identity* map—
Why should I trust you with anything else?
lines.emotion: quiet, precise, not raised — genuinely asking

lines.text: It's not rhetorical. Alex is actually waiting for an answer.
Classroom, presentation day. Wren and Alex's map — genuinely detailed, coastlines and all — waits at the front.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
lines.action: leans over, smirk, low enough for just the row

lines.text: Alex is close enough to hear. Doesn't move. Waits.
Alex double-checks the poster board corners, straightening them for the third time. Hands not quite steady.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
Alex's hands go still on the poster. Doesn't turn around. Waiting to see.
Alex hasn't said more than four words to Wren all week. Holds the poster like a shield, same as the notebook that first day.
Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom.
Alex doesn't wait to see. Already stepping back half a step, jaw tight, bracing.
Hallway after class, backpacks slung, everyone else already gone.
...Same time tomorrow?
lines.emotion: small, real smile, surprised by itself

lines.text: I want to show you the part I didn't present.
Yeah. Obviously yeah.
Alex doesn't say the word 'friends.' Doesn't have to.
Hallway, quieter than the day before. Alex adjusts the strap of their bag, doesn't quite meet Wren's eyes, but doesn't leave either.
...See you tomorrow.
lines.emotion: not quite a question, not quite settled

lines.text: Yeah. Tomorrow.
The door doesn't close. It just stays open a crack.
Cafeteria, the next day. Maya's table louder than ever, waving Wren over before she's even through the door.
There she is — sit, sit, you're basically one of us now.
Two tables over, a single seat sits empty. Notebook-shaped absence. Wren can't stop looking at it.
Hallway, days later. Alex passes by, eyes fixed straight ahead, like Wren's just part of the wall.
Hey—
lines.emotion: dies before it becomes anything else

lines.text: The project got turned in. Got a fine grade. Nobody mentioned the raccoon king again.

options.text: Stay. Laugh it off. Fix it later.

a.text: Oh! Should I — no? Okay, cool, I'll just... hold your seat, I guess.

b.text: Ha — yeah, no, Sir Fluffington's legacy lives on.
I'll catch them after lunch. Definitely. For sure.

options.text: Tell them exactly what I did, straight up — no excuses.
Say sorry, but explain I was just trying to be funny.

a.text: I took something you showed me — in confidence — and turned it into a bit for a laugh. That wasn't mine to use, and I knew it when I did it.
That's — that's actually most of what I needed you to say.
a.action: taps twice, not four, looks away

b.text: I'm sorry, okay? I didn't mean anything by it, I was just trying to get a laugh, it wasn't even about you—
It was pretty specifically about me.

b.action: turns, taps four times against the locker, tighter than before

options.text: Catch the door. Say something, even if it's rushed.
Let it go. Deal with it later.

a.text: Wren cuts through the crowd, catches the door before it swings shut.
Hey — Alex, wait, I just — I'm sorry. About lunch. That wasn't okay, what I did.

a.action: already half inside, doesn't fully turn

b.text: The door swings shut. Wren doesn't move.
...Later's going to need a lot of later's, huh.

options.text: I hear you. That's fair — thank you for telling me.
I already said sorry, what more do you want from me?

a.emotion: like they expected pushback and didn't get it

a.action: goes back to the notebook, a little looser

b.text: Forget it. Let's just do the project.
b.action: closes the notebook halfway, voice flat

b.text: Alex doesn't look up again for the rest of the session.

options.text: Admit it — I wanted to matter at that table, and it cost you something I didn't mean to spend.
It's not that big a deal, you're overreacting.

a.action: long pause, taps four times, softer this time
slides one more index card over, small, testing

a.text: Here. One more fact. See what you do with it.

b.text: Alex doesn't say another word the rest of the session.

options.text: Shut it down: 'It's actually really good — you'll see.'
Laugh it off vaguely, keep the peace.

a.text: It's actually really good. You'll see.

a.action: glances over, something loosens in the shoulders

b.action: goes still, taps four times fast on the poster edge

options.text: Hold the line, clearly, so everyone hears it.

a.text: It's a real project. We worked hard on it. You'll see.

a.action: turns, slow, searching Wren's face for the catch and not finding one
taps twice — half the usual four

b.text: I mean, it's not that bad, calm down—
b.emotion: laughs a little, doesn't fully commit

b.action: doesn't turn around, picks up the poster alone

options.text: Stop everything. Actually mean it this time.

a.text: And also — Alex, I'm sorry. Actually sorry. Not the lunch-table kind.
A few heads turn. Alex doesn't look at Wren, but doesn't step back any further either.

b.text: Wren opens her mouth. Nothing comes out.

b.emotion: taking the silence as agreement, laughing

b.action: picks up the poster alone, steps to the front without Wren

lines.text: You didn't laugh. When Maya went for it. I kept waiting for you to.
You didn't.
I know what it cost the first time. Wasn't doing that again.
...Cool.
lines.action: taps twice, not four, like the nerves finally have somewhere else to go

lines.text: Bring your own markers tomorrow. Mine are all dying.
You were kind of a mess this week. Just so you know.
Yeah. I know.
...You came back, though. Eventually.
That's something.
Honestly? Vibes are great today. No for real.
lines.action: glances at the empty seat too, a beat too long

lines.text: I got exactly what I wanted at this table. I keep checking anyway. Every day. For a seat that isn't there.
Okay, new bit — who's got something?
...Not me. Not today.
I kept thinking there'd be a better time. Turns out the window doesn't wait around forever.
Alex stopped expecting anything from me around Wednesday. I noticed. Didn't do anything about it.
Somewhere in a notebook Wren will never see again, there's a kingdom with a coastline nobody else got to hear about.

voiceNotes: Wren tests every room before she speaks in it — her humor is a survival skill, not cruelty, which is why the laugh in scene 1 has to feel earned and almost involuntary, not villainous. Her apologies should never be eloquent on the first try; real accountability (choice-2a) should feel harder to say than the deflecting version, slightly clumsy, like she's fighting her own instinct to make it a bit. Alex's guardedness is load-bearing: short sentences, the four-taps tic as an anxiety tell that changes rhythm (four = guarded, two = softening) tracks the emotional temperature scene to scene without narration having to state it. Maya is never malicious, which is what makes her dangerous — she genuinely doesn't register the cost of what she asks for, and that obliviousness should read as a specific character trait, not just plot convenience. Jordan's 'laughing before the joke lands' and late-arriving discomfort ('...Wait, was that—') are his tell for conflict he can't yet name; he should always almost say something true and then cover it with a bit. Mr. Alvarez should never lecture — his lines land through structure (the seating chart, the pairing) more than speeches; 'here's the thing' is reserved for when he actually pivots something, not overused. Branch logic assumed: choice-1a -> scene-3a; choice-1b -> scene-3b. Within 3a, choice-2a -> pairing-warm; choice-2b -> pairing-cold. Within 3b, either choice-2b outcome -> pairing-cold. Library-warm's choice-3a continues toward showdown-solid/friendship; choice-3b diverts toward popularity-cost. Library-cold's choice-3b-a is the guarded-thaw recovery toward showdown-strained/tentative; choice-3b-b continues toward fractured/broken outcomes depending on the final choice-4 variant chosen.

alternatives: Better. It's a whole kingdom — flag, royal court, the works. Ruled by a raccoon king. I'm not making that up.
Better. There's a flag. For a country that isn't real. And the king's a raccoon, apparently.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeping the exact 'Sir Fluffington the Third' beat but allowing a slightly less elaborate version in case the escalation feels too performed for a first read-through — the alt versions land the same joke with less setup, useful if the pacing needs to move faster.

alternatives: I used something you only showed me because you trusted me, and I turned it into a joke to get a laugh at a table. That wasn't fair to you, and I knew it while I was doing it.
I took your notebook — the thing you specifically didn't want made fun of — and made fun of it. On purpose. For laughs.
alternativeLines.reasoning: The core line needs to name the specific harm (confidence broken, not just 'feelings hurt') without over-explaining; alternates offer slightly different register — one more formal/complete, one more clipped and guilty — depending on how raw the delivery should feel.

alternatives: Give me one reason I should hand you anything else.
What would even be the point? You already know how this goes.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Alex's line needs to feel like a genuine, unrhetorical question rather than a mic-drop; the alternates keep the flatness but vary how much bitterness leaks through, useful depending on how much warmth is left in the cold-path relationship at this point.
